insights.insight: Ad tech charges $5–$150 CPA on probabilistic attribution; this protocol settles verified purchase confirmation at roughly $0.10 per receipt — a 50–200x cost delta driven by the receipt being the conversion event, not a modeled inference of one.

With Chrome deprecation joining Safari and Firefox, device fingerprinting under regulatory pressure, and data brokers shrinking under GDPR/CCPA, no current stack produces identity-free, cryptographically verified purchase signals across ecosystems — that gap is structurally unoccupied.

Amazon, Walmart, and Kroger each see only their own slice of a shopper's behavior; the 3,893 verified receipts in this dataset span physical retail, AI/SaaS, and dev infrastructure from a single verification layer — something no platform silo can replicate without sharing customer identity.

Nine non-metro state designations are active across 17 CBSAs, with Walmart, Kroger, Dollar General, and Home Depot dominating the receipt set — adoption is spreading via social graph, not paid acquisition, which means the geographic footprint reflects organic demand from populations that ad tech routinely underserves.

Physical retail shoppers, AI/SaaS subscribers, and developer infrastructure buyers are submitting receipts through the same verification layer simultaneously — a rare unified signal across three distinct audience segments that would otherwise require three separate data partnerships.

The underlying technology is open-standard; the moat is the accumulating dataset itself — more verified receipts increase query value, which increases reward incentive, which draws more receipts, creating a flywheel that makes early access to the pool structurally more valuable than late entry.
